content:
I’m a fairly new contributor and have been reading the 'workqueue' and 'sync/atomic' modules. I started there after noticing some incomplete safety comments, and I’ve submitted a couple of patches to improve them. For now, I plan to continue focusing on documentation and safety comments to get more comfortable with the workflow and codebase.

My long-term goal is to contribute meaningful code to the kernel. What would you recommend as the next steps for someone at this stage? Are there specific modules, types of issues, or learning paths that would help bridge the gap from documentation fixes to substantive code contributions?

If you haven't seen it yet, I have some tips at: https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#ways-to-contribute

I usually recommend reviewing patches that look interesting to you from the mailing list, because that will eventually show you places to contribute that you enjoy.

There are also a couple TODO lists from projects that may interest you -- please follow the links there!

I hope that helps.
Thank you! I will go through this carefully and will start reading the patches from the mailing list.
Sagar Taunk has marked this topic as resolved.
